subject: Mathematics

unit.unitTitle: Addition and Subtraction of Whole Numbers
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3! In this unit you will grow into a confident and accurate calculator, able to add and subtract whole numbers up to four digits. You already add and subtract small numbers with ease. Now you will learn to handle bigger numbers, to choose the smartest method for each problem, and to check your own work so that you can trust every answer you write.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use symbols to represent missing numbers or operations in equations.
Solve equations with missing numbers using inverse operations.
Apply a range of mental calculation strategies for addition and subtraction.
Use written column methods for addition and subtraction with regrouping (3- and 4-digit numbers).
Estimate answers by rounding, and check calculations using inverse operations.
Solve multi-step word problems involving addition and subtraction.
Choose the most appropriate calculation method for each situation.
Explain your mathematical reasoning clearly when you solve a problem.

concepts.title: Symbols for Missing Numbers
concepts.explanation: In mathematics we often meet a number we do not yet know. Instead of leaving a gap, we mark that gap with a symbol – a shape such as □, △ or ○, or a letter such as n. The symbol is simply a placeholder that says, ‘a number belongs here, and our job is to find it.’ An equation is a statement that two sides are equal, so whatever we put in place of the symbol must keep the two sides balanced, like a fair set of market scales. Look at the equation 5 + □ = 12. It asks: five plus *what* equals twelve? You can reason,…
concepts.example: The same symbol can stand for a different value in a different equation – in one problem □ might be 7, in another it might be 250. The symbol does not have a fixed value of its own; it simply marks the spot where the…

concepts.title: Inverse Operations – the Key to Missing Numbers
concepts.explanation: Inverse operations are operations that undo each other. Addition and subtraction are inverses: if you add 15 and then subtract 15 you are back where you started. This simple idea is the master key that unlocks every missing-number problem, so learn it well. Here are the three patterns you will use again and again:
concepts.example: If a + □ = c, then □ = c − a (subtract to undo the addition).

concepts.title: Missing Operations
concepts.explanation: Sometimes it is not a number that is missing but the operation – you must decide whether a plus or a minus belongs in the box. The way to find out is to test each operation and see which one makes the equation true. Look at 18 □ 7 = 25. Test addition: 18 + 7 = 25. That is true, so the missing operation is addition (+). To be sure, test subtraction too: 18 − 7 = 11, which is not 25, so it cannot be subtraction. Now look at 32 □ 14 = 18. Test addition: 32 + 14 = 46, which is too big. Test subtraction: 32 − 14 = 18.…
concepts.example: A helpful shortcut: if the answer is bigger than the first number, the operation is probably addition; if the answer is smaller than the first number, it is probably subtraction. In 18 □ 7 = 25 the answer 25 is bigger…

concepts.title: Using a Number Line for Missing Numbers
concepts.explanation: A number line is a helpful picture for addition and subtraction. To solve 7 + □ = 15, start at 7 and count the steps forward until you reach 15: 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15 – that is 8 steps. So □ = 8. The jump from 7 to 15 is the missing number. You can picture it like this, with the jump marked above the line:

concepts.title: Mental Strategy 1 – Breaking Numbers Down
concepts.explanation: Mental calculation means working in your head, without writing every step. It is a wonderfully useful skill for the market, the kitchen and the football pitch, where you rarely have paper to hand. The first strategy is to break a number into easier parts, usually into tens and ones, and deal with each part in turn. To work out 47 + 25 in your head, break the 25 into 20 + 5. First add the tens: 47 + 20 = 67. Then add the ones: 67 + 5 = 72. You have turned one tricky sum into two easy ones. Subtraction works the…
concepts.example: This strategy scales up to larger numbers too. To add 450 + 280, break 280 into 200 + 80: 450 + 200 = 650, then 650 + 80 = 730. Always deal with the biggest place value first, then work down to the ones. The order keeps…

concepts.title: Mental Strategy 2 – Number Bonds and Complements
concepts.explanation: Number bonds are pairs of numbers with a fixed total. The bonds to 10 (such as 7 + 3 and 6 + 4) and the bonds to 100 (such as 30 + 70 and 45 + 55) are especially powerful, because reaching a tidy 10 or 100 makes the rest of a calculation simple. Learning these bonds by heart is one of the best investments you can make in your mental maths. To add 57 + 86, notice that 57 needs just 3 more to reach the tidy number 60. Take that 3 from the 86, leaving 83, and now add the tidy numbers: 60 + 83 = 143. You have used the…
concepts.example: The idea is always the same: nudge one number up to a tidy 10 or 100 by using a bond, take the same amount from the other number to keep the total unchanged, then finish easily. This is like Hassan rearranging market…

methods.title: How to add with column addition
steps: Line up the numbers by place value.
Add the ones. If the total is 10 or more, write the ones digit and carry the ten.
Add the tens, including any carried ten, and regroup again if needed.
Continue into the hundreds and thousands, then read the answer.

methods.title: How to subtract with column subtraction
steps: Line up the numbers by place value.
Start with the ones. If the top digit is smaller, borrow one from the next column.
Add 10 to the working digit and reduce the column you borrowed from by one.
Subtract each column in turn, then read the answer.

methods.title: How to find a missing number
steps: Decide which operation is in the equation.
Use the inverse operation to undo it.
Calculate the missing number.
Substitute it back into the original equation to check.

methods.title: Mental addition by adjusting
steps: Solution: Round 299 up to 300 (that is 1 too many).
Give back the extra 1: 787 − 1 = 786.

methods.title: Column addition with regrouping
steps: Solution: Ones: 4 + 8 = 12, write 2 carry 1.
Tens: 1 + 7 + 6 = 14, write 4 carry 1.
Hundreds: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13, write 3 carry 1.
Thousands: 1 + 2 + 1 = 4.

methods.title: Column subtraction across a zero
steps: Solution: Ones: 0 − 5 needs borrowing, but the tens and hundreds are also 0, so borrow from the thousand.
This makes the number 0 hundreds, 10 tens … step by step it becomes 9 hundreds, 9 tens, 10 ones.
Now 10 − 5 = 5, then 9 − 7 = 2, then 9 − 6 = 3, then 0 in the thousands.
The answer is 325.

workedExamples.title: Missing number in an addition
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Undo the addition with subtraction: □ = 500 − 246 = 254. Check by substituting back: 246 + 254 = 500. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Missing number in a subtraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The unknown had 136 taken away to leave 364, so add it back: □ = 364 + 136 = 500. Check: 500 − 136 = 364. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Missing operation
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The answer 48 is smaller than 85, so expect subtraction. Test it: 85 − 37 = 48. True, so the missing operation is subtraction (−).

workedExamples.title: Mental addition by adjusting
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Round 299 up to 300 (that is 1 too many). 487 + 300 = 787. Give back the extra 1: 787 − 1 = 786.

workedExamples.title: Column addition with regrouping
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 4 + 8 = 12, write 2 carry 1. Tens: 1 + 7 + 6 = 14, write 4 carry 1. Hundreds: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13, write 3 carry 1. Thousands: 1 + 2 + 1 = 4. The answer is 4342.

workedExamples.title: Column subtraction across a zero
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Ones: 0 − 5 needs borrowing, but the tens and hundreds are also 0, so borrow from the thousand. This makes the number 0 hundreds, 10 tens … step by step it becomes 9 hundreds, 9 tens, 10 ones. Now 10 − 5 = 5, then 9 − 7 = 2, then 9 − 6 = 3, then 0 in the thousands. The answer is 325. Check: 325 + 675 = 1000. Correct.

workedExamples.title: Estimate, calculate, check
workedExamples.prompt: For 438 + 295: estimate, then find the exact answer, then check.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate by rounding to the nearest 100: 400 + 300 = 700. Exact: 438 + 295 = 733. Check with the inverse: 733 − 438 = 295. Correct, and 733 is close to the estimate of 700, so it is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: A multi-step word problem
workedExamples.prompt: A farmer near Kismaayo has 425 sheep and 368 cows. He sells 125 sheep and buys 57 more cows. How many animals does he have now?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Step 1, total animals at the start: 425 + 368 = 793. Step 2, apply the changes: 793 − 125 (sheep sold) + 57 (cows bought) = 668 + 57 = 725 animals. Working step by step keeps a busy problem under control.

workedExamples.prompt: Column subtraction with regrouping: a) 523 − 248 b) 915 − 467 c) 634 − 278.

workedExamples.title: Solve the missing numbers
workedExamples.prompt: Solve the missing numbers: a) □ − 27 = 53 b) 200 − □ = 125 c) □ + 145 = 300.

workedExamples.title: Four-digit column addition
workedExamples.prompt: Four-digit column addition: a) 2374 + 1968 b) 5432 + 3789 c) 7845 + 6398.

activities.title: Market Stall Totals
activities.materials: small items with price labels you write yourself (stones as ‘shillings’), paper, pencil.
steps: Set up a pretend market stall with five items, each priced between 100 and 900 shillings.
Choose three items to ‘buy’ and add their prices using the column method.
Pay with a 1000 or 2000 shilling note and work out the change by subtracting.
Check each total with the inverse operation, then swap roles with a family member.
This activity practises column addition, subtraction and checking all at once – exactly the skills a real trader uses every day.

activities.title: Missing Number Detective
steps: Each player writes five missing-number equations using □, such as □ − 145 = 255.
Swap papers and solve every equation using an inverse operation.
Mark each other’s work and explain any that were tricky.
Make the numbers bigger each round to increase the challenge.

activities.title: The Regrouping Race (Maths Game)
activities.materials: number cards 0–9 (write them yourself), paper, pencil.
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal each player six cards.
Arrange your cards into two three-digit numbers.
Add them using column addition – the player with the larger correct sum wins the round.
For a subtraction round, the player with the smaller correct difference wins. Always check with the inverse.
This game rewards careful regrouping and quick checking under a little friendly pressure.

activities.title: Estimation Station (Investigation)
activities.materials: two large jars or bowls, dry beans or small stones, paper.
steps: Fill each jar with beans and estimate how many are in each without counting.
Estimate the total of both jars by rounding your two estimates and adding.
Now count the beans in tens and add the exact totals in columns.
Compare your estimate with the exact answer. Do it three times and see if your estimates improve.

activities.title: Livestock Ledger (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a family member to tell a ‘herd story’.
steps: Start with a herd size, for example 425 goats.
A family member calls out changes: ‘bought 138’, ‘sold 96’, ‘lost 47 in the dry season’.
Keep a running total, adding or subtracting at each step in columns.
At the end, check your final total by working through all the changes a second time.
This is a real multi-step problem – exactly the kind of calculation herders and traders do all the time.

activities.title: Strategy Sort
steps: Write these calculations on separate slips: 50 − 27, 786 − 359, 594 + 399, 45 + 32, 1000 − 575.
For each slip, decide whether a mental or a written method is best.
Solve each one with the method you chose, and write one sentence explaining why you chose it.
Discuss your choices with a partner – did you agree on every one?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand regrouping best? Which mental strategy do you now reach for most often? What was the trickiest part, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Inverse Operations Rule
rules.text: Subtraction undoes addition; addition undoes subtraction. If a + box = c then box = c − a. If box − a = c then box = c + a.

rules.title: Column Method Rule
rules.text: Line up digits by place value: ones under ones, tens under tens. Always start from the ones column on the right and move left. Addition carries a ten forward. Subtraction borrows a ten back.

rules.title: Estimate and Check Rule
rules.text: Estimate by rounding each number, then calculating. Check an addition by subtracting; check a subtraction by adding.

terms: A sensible answer found by rounding
A pair of numbers with a fixed total
A problem needing more than one calculation
commonMistakes: Write the small 1 above the next column and add it
Cross out the digit and write the new smaller value
Put ones under ones and tens under tens
Rounded up to add? Subtract back. Rounded up to subtract? Add back

rounds.explanation: Ones: 4 + 8 = 12, write 2 carry 1. Tens: 1 + 7 + 6 = 14, write 4 carry 1. Hundreds: 1 + 3 + 9 = 13, write 3 carry 1. Thousands: 1 + 2 + 1 = 4. The answer is 4342.

games.title: Model Detective: Inverse operation
games.description: Practise inverse operation through four short challenges.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
rounds.explanation: Ones: 0 − 5 needs borrowing, but the tens and hundreds are also 0, so borrow from the thousand. This makes the number 0 hundreds, 10 tens … step by step it becomes 9 hundreds, 9 tens, 10 ones. Now 10 − 5 = 5, then 9 − 7 = 2, then 9 − 6 =…

selfAssessment: I can use symbols to represent missing numbers or operations in equations.
I can solve equations with missing numbers using inverse operations.
I can apply a range of mental calculation strategies for addition and subtraction.
I can use written column methods for addition and subtraction with regrouping (3- and 4-digit numbers).
I can estimate answers by rounding, and check calculations using inverse operations.
I can solve multi-step word problems involving addition and subtraction.
I can choose the most appropriate calculation method for each situation.
I can explain your mathematical reasoning clearly when you solve a problem.
